nativeRange.habitat: Broken coral rubble interspersed with outcrops of soft and stony corals; benthopelagic; depth 20–38 m

diet.notes: The Ruby Red Dragonet feeds primarily on live copepods, amphipods, and other small benthic invertebrates in the wild. In captivity, some specimens learn to accept baby brine shrimp, frozen bloodworms, glassworms, and frozen Mysis shrimp, though many individuals — particularly newly acquired fish — refuse prepared foods entirely and depend on a dense, self-replenishing live copepod population in the display tank. A connected refugium that continuously produces live prey is strongly recommended for long-term success. The tank should be mature and well-established before this species is introduced.

avoidWith: other male dragonets (conspecific males will fight)
fast-moving competitive feeders (damsels, aggressive gobies, dottybacks)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Fedtred, 1024x695. Live Synchiropus sycorax in a reef tank showing the diagnostic brilliant ruby-red body, pale blue/white spots, and the tall barred sail-like first dorsal fin; whole fish in frame, vibrant in-colour. Slightly soft under blue reef lighting but the only CC-licensed live photo available and clearly ID'd. Identical image is also on iNaturalist (public domain).

summary: The Ruby Red Dragonet is a small, brilliantly colored reef fish from the Philippines, generally considered reef-safe and peaceful. It tends to require a mature, well-established aquarium with a thriving live copepod population to feed successfully long-term.
careNotes:
The Ruby Red Dragonet (Synchiropus sycorax) is a small member of the family Callionymidae — the dragonets — formally described in 2016. Males reach roughly 4 cm standard length; females are smaller, typically around 2.7 cm. It has been reported to accept a wider variety of prepared foods than the closely related Green Mandarin (Synchiropus splendidus), though many newly acquired specimens initially refuse frozen offerings and will only eat live copepods and amphipods during the adjustment period. A refugium connected to the display tank — a separate compartment used to cultivate live copepods and amphipods — is generally advised to sustain a steady supply of prey over the long term. The display tank should be mature and well-established before this fish is introduced, as a healthy population of small benthic invertebrates must already be present.

Only one male should be kept per display tank, as males are territorial and will fight with one another. A single male can be housed successfully with one or more females; when adding multiple females, introducing them simultaneously tends to reduce conflict. The tank should be large enough to support the live pod population required to feed all individuals sustainably.

The Ruby Red Dragonet has been documented as an escape risk, and a tightly secured, escape-proof lid is strongly recommended. Like all scaleless members of Callionymidae, this species is considered highly sensitive to copper-based medications; copper treatments should never be used in any aquarium housing this fish. The species is also sold in the trade under the names Ruby Dragonet and Ruby Scooter Dragonet.
